title: File Management
summary: Linux provides a small set of core commands for creating, copying, moving, and deleting files and directories. These are the building blocks of any shell script or interactive session.
keyPoints: rm: delete a file. rm -r: delete a directory recursively. rm -f: force without prompts
cp src dst: copy a file. cp -r: copy a directory
mv src dst: move or rename a file or directory
mkdir: create a directory. mkdir -p: create nested directories in one shot
touch file: create an empty file or update its timestamp
ls -la: list all files including hidden ones with permissions

exercises.prompt: Which command moves or renames a file in Linux?
exercises.explanation: mv handles both moving (to a different directory) and renaming (within the same directory).

exercises.prompt: Complete the command to create the nested directory path /tmp/a/b/c in a single command.
exercises.explanation: Without -p, mkdir fails if the parent directory doesn't exist. -p creates all missing parents.

exercises.prompt: Write the commands to: create a file called notes.txt, copy it to backup.txt, then delete the original.
exercises.hint: touch, cp, rm — in that order.
exercises.solution:
touch notes.txt
cp notes.txt backup.txt
rm notes.txt
